findings.evidence: Resolved: handleSubmit now awaits createSubject.mutateAsync inside a try/catch at lines 31-42, so API rejection is consumed while the mutation state remains responsible for rendering the error.
findings.why_it_matters: A normal create-subject API failure can produce an unhandled Promise rejection in addition to the mutation error state, making failure behavior unreliable.
findings.required_action: Catch the mutateAsync rejection inside handleSubmit while continuing to render the mutation error state, matching the guarded mutation handlers on ClassManagementPage.

findings.evidence: Resolved: handleRegistrationChange clears stale success text, awaits the mutation in a caught async flow, and records an explicit status-specific success message rendered through FormMessage at line 139. The click handler explicitly voids the fully caught Promise.
findings.why_it_matters: After the mutation completes, especially when refetch is delayed, an admin cannot reliably distinguish a successful state transition from an action that was not applied.
findings.required_action: Add an explicit, cleared-on-next-action success state/message for the affected subject registration transition while retaining current pending and error handling.

findings.evidence: Resolved: getClasses fetches the first bounded page and then each server-reported remaining page sequentially. listAllSubjects applies the same bounded page loop, and ClassManagementPage now consumes it through useAllSubjects, so dropdown inputs are not silently limited to the first 100 rows.
findings.why_it_matters: Once the catalog contains more than 100 classes or subjects, admins cannot assign later subjects or choose later replacement classes, so valid catalog operations silently become impossible.
findings.required_action: Keep API requests bounded but make the dropdown sources complete through paged/searchable selection or controlled multi-page loading, and preserve paging metadata instead of silently truncating it.
